tip1:
THEME: You often jump into pitching or accepting a prospect's initial statement without fully understanding their underlying needs, motivations, or current situation. This limits your ability to tailor your message and address unspoken concerns.

THIS WEEK: On [April 28th], you clearly explained the Mini Mommy Tummy treatment but "did not conduct any discovery to understand the prospect's needs" — instead of just pitching, you could have asked about their goals or what prompted their inquiry. On [April 28th], when a prospect said a previous offer was unsuitable, you "immediately accepting the objection" — instead, you could have asked "What specifically made it unsuitable for you?" to uncover their true concerns.

PRACTICE: For every new inquiry call, identify 3 open-ended questions you will ask *before* detailing the promotion. Examples: "What specifically caught your eye about this promotion?" or "What are you hoping to achieve with this treatment?" or "What's most important to you when considering a med-spa treatment?"

WATCH: SPIN selling discovery questions
tip2:
THEME: You are polite and professional, but sometimes you too readily accept objections like "I'm busy" or "no time" without attempting to re-engage or propose a specific, actionable next step. This leaves the ball in the prospect's court, reducing your control over the sales process.

THIS WEEK: On [April 28th], when a prospect cited "lack of time," you "did not attempt to address this objection" — instead of suggesting they message you when ready, you could have offered to find a quick 5-minute slot later or asked "When would be a better time for a brief chat, perhaps tomorrow afternoon?" On [April 29th], when a prospect asked to call back after 5 PM, you "agreed to the callback" — instead of just waiting, you could have offered "How about I call you back at 5:15 PM today?"

PRACTICE: Create a 3-step script for common objections ("I'm busy," "No time," "Call me back"): 1) Acknowledge their statement ("I understand you're busy..."), 2) Briefly re-engage with a value statement or quick question ("...this will only take a moment, I just wanted to clarify X..."), 3) Propose a specific next step ("...or would [specific time/date] work better for a quick chat?").

WATCH: Mirror technique sales objections
tip3:
THEME: While you excel at routine confirmations, some calls present unique challenges (unexpected questions, medical concerns, or specific objections) where being prepared with accurate information or a clear process for escalation is key. Being ready for these moments will boost your confidence and effectiveness.

THIS WEEK: On [April 29th], you faced a "strong objection regarding the doctor's nationality" — instead of promising to investigate, you could have had a pre-approved statement about the qualifications of all practitioners. On [April 27th], when advising a pregnant prospect, you "should be more cautious and accurate" — instead of giving medical advice, you could have stated company policy to consult a doctor or defer treatment until after pregnancy.

PRACTICE: Review the FAQ for your services. For any question you can't answer immediately, identify who *can* answer it and the process for getting that information to the prospect. Role-play handling a "difficult" or unexpected question with a colleague.

WATCH: Chris Voss tactical empathy

tip1:
THEME: You often end calls passively, agreeing to vague follow-ups or accepting a soft "no" without establishing a clear next step. This leaves potential bookings on the table.

THIS WEEK: On 2026-04-28, you ended a call with a prospect who was busy by "politely ending the call without attempting to schedule a follow-up or offer alternative next steps" — instead of just agreeing to end, you could have offered a specific time to call back. On 2026-04-28, you "provided options for the prospect to contact the spa when ready" after they said they needed two weeks — instead, you could have proactively scheduled a follow-up call for them in two weeks.

PRACTICE: When a prospect says "I'm busy" or "Call me back later," respond with: "I understand completely. What's the best day and time for me to call you back next week, so we can ensure we connect when you have a moment?" Or, "Would it be better for me to send you a quick text with the details, and then I can follow up with a call on [specific day/time]?"

WATCH: Soft close technique
tip2:
THEME: You tend to accept initial disinterest or hesitation without probing deeper to understand the underlying reasons or re-engage the prospect. This limits your ability to address concerns or find a suitable solution.

THIS WEEK: On 2026-04-28, a prospect "immediately stated they were no longer interested" and you "acknowledged the prospect's decision" — instead of immediately accepting, you could have asked, "I understand, has something changed, or is there a specific reason for the disinterest?" On 2026-04-29, a prospect said they were "not really interested at the moment" and you "offered to send a text message" — instead, you could have asked, "What makes you say 'not really interested at the moment'?"

PRACTICE: When a prospect expresses disinterest or hesitation, use a "curiosity question" like: "I hear you, is there a specific reason you're feeling that way?" or "What's changed since you first inquired?" This helps uncover the real objection.

WATCH: Chris Voss labeling objections
tip3:
THEME: Some calls start with confusion about who you are or what service you're offering, leading to quick declines. Ensuring clarity upfront is crucial for engagement.

THIS WEEK: On 2026-04-29, a prospect was "completely confused, repeatedly asking 'Who is this?'" and you "failed to clarify the offer" — instead of just repeating, you could have paused and said, "My apologies, I'm [Your Name] from [Spa Name], calling about your inquiry for [Specific Service]." On 2026-04-28, a prospect "had trouble recalling the company and service" — instead of just moving on, you could have briefly re-introduced the spa and the core benefit of the service.

PRACTICE: At the very start of every call, use this clear opening: "Hi [Prospect Name], this is [Your Name] from [Spa Name]. I'm calling because you recently inquired about our [Specific Service/Promotion]. Does that ring a bell?"

WATCH: Cold call opening Patrick Dang

tip1:
THEME: Technical Foundation

THIS WEEK: On 2026-04-28, you had multiple calls severely hampered by audio issues, and again on 2026-04-30, where prospects couldn't understand you. Instead of struggling through, you could have proactively checked your setup.

PRACTICE: Before your first call each day, do a quick sound check. Call a colleague or use a voice recorder to ensure your microphone is clear, free of echo, and at an appropriate volume.

WATCH: Sound confident on the phone
tip2:
THEME: Uncovering Needs & Overcoming Hesitation

THIS WEEK: On 2026-04-29, a prospect said they were "still reading up," and you missed asking what information they needed. On 2026-04-29, you accepted "not interested at the moment" without probing why. On 2026-05-01, a prospect didn't remember the service, and you struggled to re-engage them. Instead of accepting initial declines, you could have asked open-ended questions.

PRACTICE: For every "not interested," "busy," or "call me back," practice asking: "I understand, is there a specific reason you're not interested right now?" or "When would be a better time for a quick chat?"

WATCH: Chris Voss labeling objections
tip3:
THEME: Explaining Value, Not Just Features

THIS WEEK: On 2026-04-29, you attempted to explain "Muffin Top Rescue" as an "EMB session," which confused the prospect. On 2026-05-01, when asked what the $350 for "Slim Sculpt" covered, you failed to provide details. Instead of using jargon or just stating a price, you could have explained the benefits and what the package includes.

PRACTICE: For your top 3 services (e.g., Laser Hair Removal, Muffin Top Rescue, Fotona Lift), write down a 30-second explanation that avoids jargon, highlights 2-3 key benefits, and clearly states what's included.

WATCH: How to pitch without sounding salesy Jeb Blount

tip1:
THEME: You're consistently missing opportunities to understand your prospects' underlying needs and concerns, leading to premature call endings and low scores in discovery.

THIS WEEK: On [Tuesday], you accepted the prospect's preference for text/Instagram without asking why or what they were hoping to achieve with the 'Titan and Tone' treatment — instead of immediately agreeing, you could have asked, "Before we switch, what specifically about the treatment caught your eye?" On [Tuesday], you accepted "hard time" without probing — instead of ending the call, you could have asked, "I understand, sometimes life gets in the way. Is there anything specific that's making it a hard time right now?"

PRACTICE: For every call this week, identify 3 open-ended questions you will ask to uncover the prospect's motivation, past experiences, or current challenges related to the treatment.

WATCH: SPIN selling discovery questions
tip2:
THEME: You're accepting initial objections at face value without probing deeper, which means you're missing chances to address real concerns or pivot the conversation.

THIS WEEK: On [Tuesday], when the prospect said they preferred text/Instagram, you immediately agreed — instead of accepting, you could have acknowledged their preference and then asked, "I can certainly send you information there, but just so I know what to focus on, what questions do you have right now?" On [Tuesday], you let "hard time" end the call — instead of letting it go, you could have used a soft probe like, "I hear you, what does 'hard time' mean for you in relation to this?"

PRACTICE: When a prospect gives an initial objection, practice a 3-step response: 1) Acknowledge ("I understand," "That makes sense"), 2) Validate ("Many people feel that way"), 3) Probe ("Could you tell me more about X?").

WATCH: Chris Voss labeling objections
tip3:
THEME: Ensuring crystal-clear communication from the outset and always establishing a clear next step are fundamental to successful calls and moving prospects forward.

THIS WEEK: On [Wednesday], there was a significant communication breakdown where the prospect couldn't understand you — instead of continuing without clarity, you could have slowed down, rephrased, and confirmed understanding. On [Tuesday], you ended the call without setting a clear next step for the text/Instagram communication — instead of just agreeing, you could have said, "Great, I'll send that over. When would be a good time for me to follow up with you there?"

PRACTICE: Before each call, practice your opening statement (name, company, purpose) out loud, focusing on clear enunciation and a slightly slower pace. For every call, identify the specific next step you will propose before you hang up.

WATCH: Sound confident on the phone

tip1:
THEME: Taking the lead in guiding the scheduling process to make it seamless for the prospect.

THIS WEEK: On [2026-05-02], you proactively checked the schedule and offered Thursday when Wednesday was closed — instead of waiting for the prospect to suggest options, you could have immediately offered 2-3 specific times for Thursday. On [2026-05-02], when the initial 4 PM slot was unavailable, you waited for the prospect to suggest options — instead of waiting, you could have immediately offered a couple of alternative dates/times based on common availability.

PRACTICE: Create a "Scheduling Flow" script: "Based on what you've told me, it sounds like [day/time] would be best. How about [Option 1] or [Option 2]?" Practice offering 2-3 specific slots immediately, guiding the prospect to a decision.

WATCH: Assumptive close Tom Hopkins
tip2:
THEME: Establishing immediate clarity and professionalism at the start of every call.

THIS WEEK: On [2026-05-02], you had to clarify the business name after the prospect asked for it — instead of waiting for the prospect to ask, you could have clearly stated the business name and your purpose upfront.

PRACTICE: Develop a 3-step opening: "Hi [Prospect Name], this is [Your Name] from [Business Name]. I'm calling about [Purpose of Call]." Practice delivering this smoothly and confidently to set a clear tone.

WATCH: Cold call opening Patrick Dang
tip3:
THEME: Breaking down complex information and addressing past concerns with empathy and precision.

THIS WEEK: On [2026-05-02], you patiently explained the laser hair removal promo details and clarified area definitions after the prospect mentioned a negative past experience — instead of just explaining, you could have used more analogies or visual language to make complex details even clearer.

PRACTICE: For common promos or complex procedures, create a "FAQ bridge" script: "Many people ask about X, and what's important to know is Y." Practice explaining one complex promo in under 30 seconds, focusing on clarity and benefits.

WATCH: How to pitch without sounding salesy Jeb Blount
